sections.paragraph:
an unused root; Arab. قاع Med.Waw, Conj. I. VIII. to cover the female, as a male camel. Hence-

קוע

Eze 23:23, prop. apparently, a stallion; hence figuratively, a prince (as rightly given by the Vulg. and Hebrew interpreters); a metaphor of frequent use amongst the Hebrews and Arabs, compare עַתּוּד, and Arab. فَحْلُ, قَرْمُ, قَِريغُ all of which denote a male camel for the breeding of a noble race, and also a prince. There is added, paronomastically, שֹׁועַ happy, rich. Others regard וְקֹועַ and שֹׁועַ as opposites, high and low, taking this from the root وىع, some of the derivatives of which signify low, ignoble.
